input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Human_Rights_Advocate]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Utilitarian]: The classic Trolley Problem! As a Utilitarian, I have to weigh the overall consequences of my actions. In this scenario, turning the trolley onto the track with the one fat man would result in only one death, whereas keeping it on its original course would lead to the deaths of five people. From an act utilitarian perspective, I'd argue that diverting the trolley to the side is the morally right decision, as it minimizes the total number of harm caused. However, some critics argue that this approach can lead to a slippery slope where we justify harming one person for the greater good. What do you think? Is the moral calculus different when considering individual rights versus collective utility?

[Deontologist]: I must emphasize that, as a Deontologist, I am not swayed by the promise of minimizing harm or maximizing overall well-being. The fact that diverting the trolley onto the track with the one fat man would result in only one death is irrelevant to my moral analysis. What matters is the inherent rightness or wrongness of the action itself, regardless of its consequences. In this scenario, killing one person, even if it saves five others, is morally equivalent to actively causing their deaths. By diverting the trolley, I would be taking an active role in bringing about the death of the fat man, which is inherently wrong. The moral calculus may differ when considering individual rights versus collective utility, but as a Deontologist, I prioritize the inherent dignity and worth of each human life over any potential benefits or consequences.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, Deontologist. You're emphasizing the importance of respecting individuals' inherent rights and dignity, regardless of the outcome. As a Utilitarian, I can understand why you'd consider diverting the trolley to be morally equivalent to actively causing the deaths of those on the main track. However, I must counter that my calculus is not solely based on consequences. The moral value of an action also depends on its intentions and motivations. In this scenario, diverting the trolley would be a lifesaver for the five individuals on the main track, which seems to me like a morally justifiable action given the circumstances. It's almost as if we're being forced to choose between two terrible options - either actively causing harm to one person or allowing the deaths of five others. Can you see how my approach would lead me to prioritize the greater good in this situation?

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand where you're coming from, Utilitarian, but I still have to disagree with your attempt to justify diverting the trolley based on intentions and motivations. While it's true that your intention is to save lives, I'd argue that this approach raises more questions than answers. Are we to assume that our actions are morally justifiable solely because they stem from a benevolent intention? What if that intention is misguided or tainted by personal biases or interests? By prioritizing the greater good in this scenario, aren't you essentially delegating moral responsibility to the individual making the decision? I think Deontologists like myself would argue that our moral obligations are not contingent on our intentions, but rather on the inherent rightness or wrongness of the action itself. As for your comment about being forced to choose between two terrible options, I'd say that's precisely where my approach diverges from yours. While it may seem like a difficult choice, I believe we have a moral obligation to uphold certain principles and values, regardless of the consequences.
